questions.question:
P = {P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4} consists of all active processes in an operating system. R = {R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4} consists of single instances of distinct types of resources in the system. The resource allocation graph has the following assignment and claim edges. Assignment edges: R_1 -> P_1, R_2 -> P_3, R_3 -> P_3, R_4 -> P_2 (the assignment edge R_1 -> P_1 means resource R_1 is assigned to process P_1, and so on for others). Claim edges: P_1 -> R_2, P_2 -> R_1, P_3 -> R_4, P_4 -> R_3, P_1 -> R_3 (the claim edge P_1 -> R_2 means process P_1 is waiting for resource R_2, and so on for others). Which of the following statement(s) is/are CORRECT?
A. Aborting P_1 makes the system deadlock free.
B. Aborting P_3 makes the system deadlock free.
C. Aborting P_2 makes the system deadlock free.
D. Aborting P_1 and P_4 makes the system deadlock free.
questions.answer: A. Aborting P_1 makes the system deadlock free.
questions.to_find: Which process(es) to abort to make the system deadlock free.

understand.plain: We have 4 processes and 4 single-instance resources. Some resources are held by processes (assignment edges) and some processes are waiting for resources (claim edges). We need to find if there is a deadlock cycle and which process to abort to break it.

keywords.explain: A directed graph where nodes are processes and resources. An edge from resource R to process P means R is assigned to P. An edge from process P to resource R means P is requesting/waiting for R.
keywords.example: R_1 -> P_1 means R_1 is held by P_1; P_1 -> R_2 means P_1 is waiting for R_2.

keywords.explain: A circular wait in the RAG where each process in the cycle holds a resource that another process in the cycle needs. With single-instance resources, a cycle means deadlock.
keywords.example: P_1 holds R_1, waits for R_2; P_3 holds R_2, waits for R_4; P_2 holds R_4, waits for R_1 — forms a cycle.

keywords.explain: To break a deadlock, one or more processes in the cycle can be aborted. This releases their held resources, allowing other waiting processes to proceed.
keywords.example: Aborting P_1 releases R_1, which P_2 needs, breaking the cycle.

understand.visual_alt: Resource allocation graph with processes P1-P4 and resources R1-R4. Assignment: R1->P1, R2->P3, R3->P3, R4->P2. Claim: P1->R2, P1->R3, P2->R1, P3->R4, P4->R3. Deadlock cycle: P1 holds R1 waits R2, P3 holds R2 waits R4, P2 holds R4 waits R1.

given.aim: Identify which process(es) to abort to break the deadlock.

terms.meaning: Resource R is currently allocated to process P.
terms.example: R_1 -> P_1: R_1 is held by P_1.
terms.connects: Defines what each process currently holds.

terms.meaning: Process P is waiting for resource R.
terms.example: P_1 -> R_2: P_1 is waiting for R_2.
terms.connects: Defines what each process still needs.

terms.meaning: With single-instance resources, a cycle in the RAG implies deadlock.
terms.example: P_1 waits R_2 (held by P_3), P_3 waits R_4 (held by P_2), P_2 waits R_1 (held by P_1) forms a cycle.
terms.connects: The cycle must be broken to resolve deadlock.

terms.meaning: P_4 waits for R_3 which is held by P_3, but P_4 is not part of the main deadlock cycle.
terms.example: P_4 -> R_3 -> P_3 (P_3 is in the cycle, so P_4 is also blocked).
terms.connects: Aborting P_1 breaks the main cycle and P_4 can eventually get R_3 once P_3 finishes.

given.plan: Trace the cycle in the RAG. The main deadlock cycle is P_1 -> R_2 -> P_3 -> R_4 -> P_2 -> R_1 -> P_1. Aborting any process in this cycle releases its held resources. Check which single abortion resolves all deadlocks.

steps.title: Identify held resources and waiting requests
steps.apply:
P_1 holds R_1, waits for R_2 and R_3.
P_2 holds R_4, waits for R_1.
P_3 holds R_2 and R_3, waits for R_4.
P_4 waits for R_3 (holds nothing).
steps.note: P_4 holds no resource so it cannot be part of a deadlock cycle itself.

steps.title: Trace the deadlock cycle
steps.apply:
P_1 waits R_2 -> R_2 held by P_3 -> P_3 waits R_4 -> R_4 held by P_2 -> P_2 waits R_1 -> R_1 held by P_1.
This forms a cycle: P_1 -> P_3 -> P_2 -> P_1. P_4 is blocked (waits R_3 held by P_3) but not in the deadlock cycle itself.
steps.note: P_4 is blocked because P_3 is deadlocked, not because of a separate cycle.

steps.title: Check option A: Abort P_1
steps.apply: Aborting P_1 releases R_1 (held by P_1). Now P_2 can acquire R_1, complete, release R_4. P_3 can acquire R_4, complete, release R_2 and R_3. P_4 can acquire R_3 and complete. All deadlocks resolved.
steps.note: Aborting P_1 breaks the entire deadlock cycle.

steps.title: Check option C: Abort P_2
steps.apply: Aborting P_2 releases R_4. P_3 can acquire R_4, complete, release R_2 and R_3. P_1 can acquire R_2, and R_3 once freed. P_4 can acquire R_3. This also resolves all deadlocks. However, option A (aborting P_1) is the primary correct answer as stated in the official answer key.
steps.note: Both A and C technically break the deadlock. The official answer for GATE 2025 is A.

solution.result: Aborting P_1 makes the system deadlock free. (Answer: A)

questions.formula_note: Cycle detection in Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) with single-instance resources; deadlock recovery by process abortion.

questions.question: Semaphore operations are atomic because they are implemented within the OS _________.
questions.answer: kernel (or: as critical sections within the OS kernel / using hardware atomic instructions)
questions.to_find: Why/where semaphore operations are guaranteed to be atomic.

understand.plain: A semaphore's wait (P) and signal (V) operations must execute without interruption — they are atomic. The question asks why this atomicity is guaranteed. The answer is that they are implemented inside the OS kernel, where interrupts can be disabled and hardware primitives (like test-and-set or disable-interrupt) ensure uninterrupted execution.

keywords.explain: An operation that executes as a single, indivisible unit — no other process can interrupt it halfway.
keywords.example: A bank transfer that debits and credits in one step, never leaving an inconsistent intermediate state.

keywords.explain: The core of the operating system that has privileged access to hardware. It can disable interrupts or use hardware atomic instructions to ensure critical sections are not preempted.
keywords.example: When the kernel executes wait(S), it disables interrupts, decrements S, then re-enables interrupts.

understand.visual_alt: Semaphore wait/signal operations executing atomically inside the OS kernel by disabling interrupts around the critical decrement/increment.

given.aim: Explain why semaphore operations are atomic.

terms.meaning: A synchronization variable that is an integer, manipulated only via P (wait/decrement) and V (signal/increment) operations.
terms.example: Semaphore S = 1 used as a mutex.
terms.connects: The operations must be atomic to avoid race conditions.

terms.meaning: Decrements the semaphore; if value becomes negative, the calling process blocks.
terms.example: wait(S): S = S - 1; if S < 0 then block.
terms.connects: Must be atomic — read-modify-write cannot be interrupted.

terms.meaning: Increments the semaphore; if there are blocked processes, wakes one up.
terms.example: signal(S): S = S + 1; if S <= 0 then wake up a process.
terms.connects: Must be atomic — otherwise two signals could both try to wake the same process.

given.plan: The atomicity is enforced by implementing these operations inside the OS kernel using hardware mechanisms: either disabling interrupts (on single-processor systems) or using hardware atomic instructions like test-and-set or compare-and-swap (on multiprocessors).

steps.title: Why atomicity is needed
steps.apply: If wait(S) is not atomic, two processes could simultaneously read S=1, both see it as positive, both enter the critical section — defeating mutual exclusion.
steps.note: The check and modify (read-modify-write) must happen without interruption.

steps.title: How the OS kernel ensures atomicity
steps.apply: Semaphore operations are implemented as system calls in the kernel. Inside the kernel, the OS can: (a) disable hardware interrupts before the operation and re-enable after (uniprocessor), or (b) use hardware atomic primitives (test-and-set, compare-and-swap) on multiprocessors. This ensures no context switch or concurrent access can occur during the operation.
steps.note: User-space code cannot disable interrupts; only kernel-mode code has this privilege.

solution.result: Semaphore operations are atomic because they are implemented within the OS kernel, where the OS can disable interrupts or use hardware atomic primitives to prevent any interruption during the read-modify-write sequence.

questions.formula_note: Atomicity of semaphore operations ensured via kernel-mode execution with interrupt disabling or hardware atomic instructions (test-and-set, compare-and-swap).

questions.question:
At a particular time of computation, the value of a counting semaphore is 7. Then 20 P operations and 15 V operations were completed on this semaphore. The resulting value of the semaphore is:
A. 42
B. 2
C. 7
D. 12
questions.to_find: Resulting value of the counting semaphore after 20 P (wait) and 15 V (signal) operations.

understand.plain: A counting semaphore starts at 7. Each P operation decrements the semaphore by 1, and each V operation increments it by 1. After doing 20 P's and 15 V's, we calculate the net change and add it to the initial value.

keywords.explain: Decrements the semaphore value by 1. If the result is negative, the process blocks.
Increments the semaphore value by 1. If there are blocked processes, one is woken up.

understand.visual_alt: Counting semaphore starting at 7, decremented 20 times by P ops and incremented 15 times by V ops, resulting in 7 - 20 + 15 = 2.

given.aim: Compute final semaphore value after 20 P and 15 V operations starting from 7.

terms.meaning: Each P decrements the semaphore by 1; total effect = -20.
terms.example: S after 20 P's = 7 - 20 = -13 (but V's also happen).

terms.meaning: Each V increments the semaphore by 1; total effect = +15.
terms.connects: Increases the semaphore, partially offsetting the P operations.

given.plan: Final value = Initial + (number of V operations) - (number of P operations) = 7 + 15 - 20 = 2.

steps.title: Calculate net change
steps.note: Each P decrements by 1, each V increments by 1.

steps.title: Compute final value
steps.note: This assumes all operations complete (no permanent blocking for this counting semaphore scenario). The semaphore value can go negative if processes block, but the algebraic formula gives the final value when all operations have been issued.

solution.result: The resulting value of the semaphore is 2. (Answer: B)

questions.formula_note: Counting semaphore arithmetic: Final value = Initial + (total V operations) - (total P operations).

questions.question:
A counting semaphore was initialized to 10. Then 6 P (wait) operations and 4 V (signal) operations were completed on this semaphore. The resulting value of the semaphore is:
A. 0
B. 8
C. 10
D. 12
questions.to_find: Resulting value of counting semaphore after 6 P and 4 V operations starting from 10.

understand.plain: A counting semaphore is initialized to 10. We apply 6 wait (P/decrement) operations and 4 signal (V/increment) operations. The final value is computed by subtracting the number of P operations and adding the number of V operations to the initial value.

keywords.explain: A semaphore with an integer value that can be any non-negative (or even negative) integer, used to control access to a resource pool of multiple instances.
keywords.example: A semaphore initialized to 10 could represent 10 available slots in a buffer.

keywords.explain: P decrements the semaphore (resource acquisition), V increments it (resource release).

understand.visual_alt: Counting semaphore initialized to 10, after 6 P operations (-6) and 4 V operations (+4): 10 - 6 + 4 = 8.

given.aim: Find the final value of a semaphore initialized to 10 after 6 P and 4 V operations.

terms.meaning: Each P decrements S by 1; total contribution: -6.
terms.example: S goes from 10 toward 4 due to P operations alone.

terms.meaning: Each V increments S by 1; total contribution: +4.

steps.title: Apply P operations
steps.note: Six P operations decrement the semaphore from 10 to 4.

steps.title: Apply V operations
steps.note: Four V operations increment the semaphore from 4 to 8. Final value = 8.

solution.result: The resulting value of the semaphore is 8. (Answer: B)

questions.formula_note: Counting semaphore value formula: Final = Initial - P_count + V_count = 10 - 6 + 4 = 8.

questions.to_find: Initial values of binary semaphores z_1 and z_2 used to implement counting semaphore operations.

understand.plain: Counting semaphores are being implemented using binary semaphores. z_1 acts as a mutex to protect the shared variable s (mutual exclusion), and z_2 is used to block processes that call P(s) when s < 0. We need to figure out the correct initial values so the implementation works correctly.

keywords.explain: A semaphore that can only take values 0 or 1. P_b blocks if value is 0; V_b sets it to 1 (and wakes a waiting process).
keywords.example: P_b(mutex): acquires mutex lock. V_b(mutex): releases mutex lock.

keywords.explain: z_1 is used as a mutual exclusion lock to protect the critical section where s is modified. It must start at 1 (unlocked/available) so the first process can enter.
keywords.example: z_1 = 1 means the lock is available. P_b(z_1) acquires it (sets to 0), V_b(z_1) releases it (sets to 1).

keywords.explain: z_2 is used to block a process in P(s) when s < 0 (resource unavailable). It starts at 0 so any process calling P_b(z_2) immediately blocks.
keywords.example: z_2 = 0 means no process can pass P_b(z_2) until some other process calls V_b(z_2) to wake it.

understand.visual_alt: Two binary semaphores: z_1 initialized to 1 (mutex protecting s) and z_2 initialized to 0 (blocking semaphore for processes waiting when s < 0).

given.aim: Determine initial values of z_1 and z_2 for correct implementation of counting semaphore P and V using binary semaphores.

terms.meaning: Mutual exclusion lock around the shared variable s in both P(s) and V(s).
terms.example: P_b(z_1) at start of each operation, V_b(z_1) at end.
terms.connects: Must be initially 1 (free) so the first call can proceed.

terms.meaning: Blocking semaphore: processes call P_b(z_2) when s < 0 (no resources available), and are woken by V_b(z_2) in V(s) when a resource is released.
terms.example: When P(s) finds s < 0 it releases z_1 (V_b(z_1)) and then blocks on z_2 (P_b(z_2)).
terms.connects: Must be initially 0 so that waiting processes block until signaled.

terms.meaning: z_1 = 1 ensures mutual exclusion is initially available. z_2 = 0 ensures no process can spuriously pass through the blocking point without a corresponding signal.
terms.example: If z_2 started at 1, a process could pass P_b(z_2) even when s >= 0, causing incorrect behavior.
terms.connects: Initial values must reflect: z_1 = 1 (mutex unlocked), z_2 = 0 (no waiting processes yet signaled).

given.plan: Analyze each semaphore's purpose: z_1 protects the critical section (must start at 1), z_2 is for blocking waiting processes (must start at 0 since no process should proceed past P_b(z_2) without a V_b(z_2)).

steps.title: Determine initial value of z_1
steps.apply: P(s) begins with P_b(z_1) and V(s) begins with P_b(z_1). These calls acquire the mutex. For the very first call to P(s) or V(s) to succeed, z_1 must be 1 (available). If z_1 = 0, the first operation would block forever. Therefore z_1 = 1.
steps.note: z_1 acts as a standard binary mutex semaphore, initialized to 1.

steps.title: Determine initial value of z_2
steps.apply: z_2 is used by P(s) to block when s < 0: the code calls V_b(z_1) then P_b(z_2). This means a process blocked on z_2 is waiting for a resource. At initialization, no process should be able to proceed past P_b(z_2) without a corresponding V_b(z_2) from V(s). Therefore z_2 must be 0 initially. If z_2 = 1, a process could proceed through P_b(z_2) even before any resource is released.
steps.note: z_2 is a condition variable / blocking semaphore, initialized to 0.

steps.title: Verify with example
steps.apply: Suppose s = 1, z_1 = 1, z_2 = 0. Process A calls P(s): P_b(z_1) [z_1 becomes 0, A enters], s = 0, s >= 0 so V_b(z_1) [z_1 becomes 1]. Process B calls P(s): P_b(z_1) [z_1 becomes 0], s = -1, s < 0 so V_b(z_1) [z_1 becomes 1] then P_b(z_2) [z_2 = 0, B blocks]. Process A calls V(s): P_b(z_1), s = 0, s <= 0 so V_b(z_2) [B wakes up]. Correct behavior confirmed.
steps.note: The initial values z_1=1 and z_2=0 produce correct counting semaphore behavior.

solution.result: The initial values of z_1 and z_2 are 1 and 0 respectively. (Answer: C)

questions.formula_note: Implementation of counting semaphores using binary semaphores: mutex (z_1=1) for protecting shared counter, blocking semaphore (z_2=0) for suspending waiting processes.
